title: Farmhouse Washer & Dryer Pedestals
summary: A furniture-style appliance pedestal built like a tiny house floor — 2x4 joists, plywood decking, 4x4 posts — because store pedestals cost more than they should and hold less.
description:
Appliance-brand pedestals are a couple hundred dollars each and give you one shallow drawer. This build raises both machines to a comfortable height on what is honestly just two miniature floor systems: a 2x4 joist frame decked in 3/4" plywood up top, 4x4 posts, and a second 'floor' at the bottom that doubles as a laundry-basket shelf. Base cap moulding around the posts turns structure into furniture.

Respect what this thing has to carry. A washer full of water and spinning laundry is heavy AND alive — the source builder's machine 'stomps out of the laundry room' on overload, which is why the corner angle brackets in step 3 exist. They're cheap insurance against a load that actively shakes its platform. Build the top floor with all its center joists; the bottom floor can skip them because baskets don't weigh what water does.

Step 7's bracing options are there for the same reason — if the finished pedestal shimmies with your particular machine, skin the back in plywood scraps (glued and screwed to posts and framing, it becomes a shear wall) or screw the frame to the wall studs. Do the wall attachment anyway if small kids live in the house.

Use a level during assembly and shim the floor contact if your laundry room slab slopes (they usually do, toward the drain). A washer on an unlevel pedestal walks; a washer on a level one stays put. MDF is fine if the pedestal will be painted and the laundry room stays dry; plywood shrugs off the occasional overflow better.

tools.note: Joists, posts, and the moulding miters.
Pilot the angled screws into the 4x4s.
1" brads for the base cap moulding.
A washer on an unlevel pedestal walks. Level and shim during assembly, not after.
1-1/2" setting with 2-1/2" screws is a clean alternative for the frame joints.

materials.name: Plywood or MDF, 3/4", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Decking both floors. Plywood survives laundry-room water events better; MDF paints smoother.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Joists and rims for both floors.

materials.name: 4x4 post, 4 ft
materials.note: The four legs. Two 2x4s glued and screwed face-to-face substitute — add one 2x4 to the list.

materials.name: Base cap moulding, 6 ft
materials.note: Wraps the posts — the 'farmhouse furniture' part. Optional but it's what sells the look.

materials.name: Angle brackets
materials.note: Corner insurance against a machine that shakes on spin cycle.

cutList.note: All of them in the top floor; the bottom floor can skip its center joists — baskets are light.
Two per floor. 60" fits a standard washer + dryer pair — measure your machines plus an inch of breathing room.
Or laminate pairs of 2x4s. 11" puts the drum at a no-stoop height; adjust to your back.
Cut to fit around each post, mitered at the corners.

steps.title: Frame both floors
steps.body:
Build two mini floor systems from the 2x4s with glue and 3" screws (or 1-1/2" pocket holes with 2-1/2" pocket screws): rims front and back, joists between. The TOP floor gets every joist — it carries two running machines. The bottom floor can skip its center joists; laundry baskets don't earn them.

Square each frame by matching diagonals before the glue sets.

steps.title: Deck the floors
steps.body: Glue and screw the 3/4" decking to each frame. Glue matters here — a glued deck turns the frame into a torsion box, and torsional stiffness is exactly what fights a shaking washer.

steps.title: Bracket the corners
steps.body: Screw an angle bracket into each inside corner of the top floor with the 2" screws. The source builder added these because an overloaded machine literally walks — the brackets are cheap and this is the step where they're easy. Don't skip them because your current washer is polite; your next one may not be.

steps.title: Attach the posts to the top floor
steps.body: Flip the top floor deck-down and screw a 4x4 post into each corner through the framing with 3" screws. Pilot-drill and add a few screws driven at an angle into the post from the frame faces — angled screws resist the racking that straight-down screws allow.

steps.title: Land it on the bottom floor
steps.body:
Set the post assembly onto the bottom floor and screw UP through the bottom deck into each post — at least three 3" screws per leg.

Now level the whole pedestal in place and shim any floor gap; laundry slabs slope toward the drain, and the machine's feet can only correct so much.

steps.title: Trim, brace if needed, load
steps.body:
Miter the base cap moulding around each post with 1" brads — the ten-minute step that turns framing lumber into furniture. Sand and paint.

Set the machines, run a spin cycle with a full load, and watch. Any shimmy: skin the back in 3/4" scrap (glue + 3" screws every 4" into posts and framing — it becomes a shear panel) and/or screw the frame to wall studs. With kids in the house, do the stud attachment regardless.

images.alt: Washer and dryer raised on a farmhouse-style wooden pedestal with basket shelf below
